citation: Biology 206: Gene Mutation & Chromosomes (Chapters 12 & 13) - McGraw Hill

questions.question: What distinguishes a 'missense mutation' from a 'nonsense mutation' at the molecular level?
options: a- Missense mutations affect RNA while nonsense mutations affect DNA
b- Missense replaces one amino acid with another; nonsense converts amino acid codon to stop codon producing truncated protein
c- Nonsense mutations are silent and have no effect on protein
d- Missense mutations only occur in males; nonsense only in females
e- Both mutations produce identical phenotypic effects
questions.answer: b- Missense replaces one amino acid with another; nonsense converts amino acid codon to stop codon producing truncated protein

questions.question: How do palindromic sequences cause larger insertion/deletion mutations compared to short repetitive sequences?
options: a- Palindromes are longer than repetitive sequences so they contain more mutations
b- Complementary pairing when palindromes unwind interferes with replication causing larger indels than polymerase slippage at short repeats
c- Palindromes activate different DNA repair mechanisms
d- Palindromes only cause deletions while short repeats cause insertions
e- Palindromes prevent DNA replication from starting
questions.answer: b- Complementary pairing when palindromes unwind interferes with replication causing larger indels than polymerase slippage at short repeats

questions.question: What is 'gonadal mosaicism' in the context of germline mutations?
options: a- All body cells in an individual carry the same germline mutation
b- Germline mutations that don't affect reproductive function
c- Some gonadal stem cells spontaneously mutate producing mixed populations of gametes with and without mutations
d- A condition where the gonad develops two different sexes
e- Mosaicism that only occurs in females, not males
questions.answer: c- Some gonadal stem cells spontaneously mutate producing mixed populations of gametes with and without mutations

questions.question: Why do expansion repeat disorders (like myotonic dystrophy) worsen with each generation?
options: a- Environmental toxins increase with each generation
b- Each generation gains more lifestyle risk factors
c- Trinucleotide repeats expand, producing longer toxic proteins that bind transcription factors and block proteasomes
d- The disease is only expressed after age 40
e- Mutations are only passed through the paternal line
questions.answer: c- Trinucleotide repeats expand, producing longer toxic proteins that bind transcription factors and block proteasomes

questions.question: What is 'somatic mosaicism' and how does it differ from germline mosaicism?
options: a- Both types affect all body cells equally
b- Somatic mosaicism: mutation in some body cells during mitosis (not inherited); germline: mutation in some gametes during meiosis (inherited)
c- Somatic mosaicism only affects the brain
d- Germline mosaicism cannot be passed to offspring
e- Somatic mosaicism is more severe than germline mosaicism
questions.answer: b- Somatic mosaicism: mutation in some body cells during mitosis (not inherited); germline: mutation in some gametes during meiosis (inherited)

questions.question: What causes meiosis I nondisjunction versus meiosis II nondisjunction, and how do the resulting gametes differ?
options: a- Both meiosis I and II nondisjunction produce identical aneuploid gametes
b- Meiosis I: both homologs enter same cell (one gets both, other gets none); Meiosis II: both sister chromatids in one cell (one gets both, other gets none)
c- Only meiosis I nondisjunction produces viable aneuploid gametes
d- Meiosis II nondisjunction cannot occur in females
e- Both produce identical results but at different times in development
questions.answer: b- Meiosis I: both homologs enter same cell (one gets both, other gets none); Meiosis II: both sister chromatids in one cell (one gets both, other gets none)

questions.question: A female with Turner syndrome (45,X) resulted from nondisjunction. Was the nondisjunction in meiosis I or II?
options: a- Both are equally likely to produce Turner syndrome
b- Meiosis I nondisjunction in sperm; meiosis II nondisjunction in egg can produce nullisomic gametes
c- Turner syndrome can only result from maternal nondisjunction
d- Turner syndrome results from paternal meiosis I nondisjunction producing nullisomic sperm (no sex chromosome)
e- Turner syndrome cannot result from nondisjunction
questions.answer: d- Turner syndrome results from paternal meiosis I nondisjunction producing nullisomic sperm (no sex chromosome)

questions.question: Why are Down syndrome (trisomy 21) and other autosomal trisomies viable while most autosomal monosomies are lethal?
options: a- Trisomies have genes that prevent miscarriage
b- Monosomy causes loss of essential genes creating severe imbalance; trisomy 21 viable because chromosome 21 has fewer genes
c- All monosomies and trisomies have equal lethality
d- Autosomal trisomies are always fatal in utero
e- Monosomies only affect males while trisomies affect females
questions.answer: b- Monosomy causes loss of essential genes creating severe imbalance; trisomy 21 viable because chromosome 21 has fewer genes

questions.question: What does chromosomal shorthand '47,XX,+21' indicate about an individual?
options: a- Male with 47 chromosomes and normal autosomes
b- Female with 46 chromosomes and one deleted autosome
c- Female with 47 chromosomes and an extra chromosome 21 (Down syndrome)
d- Female with 45 chromosomes and Turner syndrome
e- Individual with three sex chromosomes
questions.answer: c- Female with 47 chromosomes and an extra chromosome 21 (Down syndrome)

questions.question: How does cell-free fetal DNA (cfDNA) testing provide noninvasive chromosomal abnormality detection?
options: a- cfDNA requires amniocentesis to obtain samples
b- ~20% of pregnant mother's blood DNA comes from placenta; entire fetal genome reconstructed without infection/miscarriage risk
c- cfDNA can only detect Down syndrome, not other aneuploidies
d- cfDNA testing requires fetal tissue sampling
e- cfDNA cannot detect structural chromosomal abnormalities
questions.answer: b- ~20% of pregnant mother's blood DNA comes from placenta; entire fetal genome reconstructed without infection/miscarriage risk

questions.question: What is a 'translocation' and how does it differ from other structural chromosome mutations?
options: a- Translocation is deletion of a chromosome segment
b- Translocation: two chromosomes join/exchange parts; differs from deletion (loss) inversion (reversal) duplication (present twice)
c- Translocation only affects sex chromosomes
d- Translocation is always lethal
e- Translocation prevents all cell division
questions.answer: b- Translocation: two chromosomes join/exchange parts; differs from deletion (loss) inversion (reversal) duplication (present twice)

questions.question: What is the relationship between mutation hotspots and duplicated genes during crossing over?
options: a- Duplicated genes cannot undergo crossing over
b- Duplicated genes located nearby are hotspots; misalignment during crossing over causes unequal recombination (one with extra copies, other with loss)
c- Duplicated genes always cause translocations
d- Crossing over cannot occur near duplicated genes
e- Duplicated genes prevent mutations entirely
questions.answer: b- Duplicated genes located nearby are hotspots; misalignment during crossing over causes unequal recombination (one with extra copies, other with loss)

questions.question: How do transposons ('jumping genes') cause mutations when they insert into new genomic locations?
options: a- Transposons only cause beneficial mutations
b- Disrupt insertion site origin, shut off target gene transcription if they insert in promoter, or alter reading frame if not multiple of 3 bases
c- Transposons always increase gene expression
d- Transposons cannot affect nearby genes
e- Transposon movement is always detected and corrected
questions.answer: b- Disrupt insertion site origin, shut off target gene transcription if they insert in promoter, or alter reading frame if not multiple of 3 bases

questions.question: What is a 'copy number variant' (CNV) and what determines whether it affects phenotype?
options: a- CNVs always disrupt gene function
b- CNVs are sequences varying in copy numbers between individuals; phenotypic effect depends on whether they disrupt genes or are neutral
c- CNVs only occur in repetitive DNA regions
d- All CNVs are harmful and cause disease
e- CNVs cannot be detected using forensic techniques
questions.answer: b- CNVs are sequences varying in copy numbers between individuals; phenotypic effect depends on whether they disrupt genes or are neutral

questions.question: What is the relationship between 'del', 'dup', 'inv', and 'trans' in chromosomal nomenclature?
options: a- All refer to the same type of chromosomal mutation
b- Deletion, Duplication, Inversion, Translocation - different structural chromosome mutations each with distinct mechanisms
c- These terms only apply to sex chromosomes
d- They describe the same mutation occurring at different times
e- Only deletion and duplication are actual mutations; inversion and translocation are normal processes
questions.answer: b- Deletion, Duplication, Inversion, Translocation - different structural chromosome mutations each with distinct mechanisms

questions.question: Why is chromosomal abnormality responsible for ~50% of spontaneous abortions yet only 0.65% of newborns have them?
options: a- Chromosomal abnormalities are beneficial and increase survival
b- Most chromosomal abnormalities abort before birth; only less severe ones survive to birth
c- Chromosomal abnormalities cannot affect newborns
d- All newborns have chromosomal abnormalities but they don't show symptoms
e- Chromosomal abnormalities develop after birth, not during pregnancy
questions.answer: b- Most chromosomal abnormalities abort before birth; only less severe ones survive to birth

questions.question: What is an 'ideogram' and what information does it provide about chromosomes?
options: a- Ideogram is a type of mutation
b- Diagrammatic representation of chromosome showing p (short) and q (long) arms with numbered bands/sub-bands identifying specific genes
c- Ideogram shows only the centromere location
d- Ideograms are used exclusively in karyotype preparations
e- Ideograms cannot identify gene locations
questions.answer: b- Diagrammatic representation of chromosome showing p (short) and q (long) arms with numbered bands/sub-bands identifying specific genes

questions.question: How does the X-inactivation therapeutic approach potentially treat trisomy 21 (Down syndrome)?
options: a- Adds extra chromosome 21 to all body cells
b- Uses X-inactivation mechanism to 'turn off' extra chromosome 21, silencing extra genetic information
c- Completely removes chromosome 21
d- Trisomy 21 cannot be treated with any gene therapy
e- X-inactivation only works on sex chromosomes, not autosomes
questions.answer: b- Uses X-inactivation mechanism to 'turn off' extra chromosome 21, silencing extra genetic information

questions.question: What percentage of spontaneous mutations occur in 'mutational hotspots' versus random locations in DNA?
options: a- All mutations occur randomly with equal probability
b- Mutations preferentially occur in hotspots (short repeats, palindromes, duplicated genes) rather than random locations
c- Mutations never occur in hotspots
d- Hotspots account for less than 1% of all mutations
e- Mutation location is determined entirely by environmental exposure
questions.answer: b- Mutations preferentially occur in hotspots (short repeats, palindromes, duplicated genes) rather than random locations
